content.text: are the primary interface between a user's question and an LlamaIndex index. They orchestrate the full pipeline: retrieve relevant chunks from an index, optionally filter or re-rank them with postprocessors, and synthesise a final response using the LLM. Each index exposes
as a convenience factory, but you can also compose query engines from individual components for full control.
The fastest way to query an index is to call

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What is the main purpose of this document?")

print(response.response)
for node in response.source_nodes:
    print(f"  Score: {node.score:.3f}  Text: {node.text[:80]}")
to control retrieval depth, response mode, and postprocessors.

from llama_index.core.postprocessor import SimilarityPostprocessor

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(
    similarity_top_k=8,                   # retrieve 8 candidates before filtering
    response_mode="tree_summarize",        # hierarchical synthesis
    node_postprocessors=[
        SimilarityPostprocessor(similarity_cutoff=0.7),  # drop low-score nodes
    ],
    streaming=False,
    verbose=False,
)
Number of nodes retrieved from the vector store
How the LLM synthesises across retrieved nodes
List of postprocessors applied after retrieval
True prints debug information during the query
For full control over each pipeline stage, build the query engine from components explicitly.

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.core.query_engine import RetrieverQueryEngine
from llama_index.core.retrievers import VectorIndexRetriever
from llama_index.core.response_synthesizers import get_response_synthesizer
from llama_index.core.postprocessor import SimilarityPostprocessor, KeywordNodePostprocessor

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)

retriever = VectorIndexRetriever(index=index, similarity_top_k=10)

postprocessors = [
    SimilarityPostprocessor(similarity_cutoff=0.72),
    KeywordNodePostprocessor(required_keywords=["python"]),
]

synthesiser = get_response_synthesizer(response_mode="compact")

query_engine = RetrieverQueryEngine(
    retriever=retriever,
    response_synthesizer=synthesiser,
    node_postprocessors=postprocessors,
)

response = query_engine.query("Explain Python decorators.")
print(response)
decomposes a complex question into sub-questions, routes each to the appropriate query engine, and aggregates the answers. It is designed for questions that span multiple topics or data sources.

from llama_index.core.tools import QueryEngineTool
from llama_index.core.query_engine import SubQuestionQueryEngine
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI

# Two separate document sets
q1_docs = SimpleDirectoryReader("./q1_reports").load_data()
q2_docs = SimpleDirectoryReader("./q2_reports").load_data()

q1_engine = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(q1_docs).as_query_engine()
q2_engine = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(q2_docs).as_query_engine()

tools = [
    QueryEngineTool.from_defaults(
        query_engine=q1_engine,
        name="q1_reports",
        description="Q1 2024 financial and operational reports.",
    ),
    QueryEngineTool.from_defaults(
        query_engine=q2_engine,
        name="q2_reports",
        description="Q2 2024 financial and operational reports.",
    ),
]

sub_question_engine = SubQuestionQueryEngine.from_defaults(
    query_engine_tools=tools,
    llm=OpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),
    verbose=True,
)

response = sub_question_engine.query(
    "Compare Q1 and Q2 revenue growth and identify the biggest change."
)
print(response)
The SubQuestionQueryEngine generates sub-questions and issues them in parallel. Set
(the default) to benefit from concurrent sub-question execution.
selects a single best query engine from a set of candidates using a LLM-based router. It is useful when different indexes serve different types of questions.

from llama_index.core.query_engine.router_query_engine import RouterQueryEngine
from llama_index.core.selectors import LLMSingleSelector
from llama_index.core.tools import QueryEngineTool

# Two specialised query engines
hr_engine = hr_index.as_query_engine()
tech_engine = tech_index.as_query_engine()

tools = [
    QueryEngineTool.from_defaults(
        query_engine=hr_engine,
        description="Use for HR, benefits, onboarding, and people-related questions.",
    ),
    QueryEngineTool.from_defaults(
        query_engine=tech_engine,
        description="Use for technical architecture, API docs, and engineering processes.",
    ),
]

router_engine = RouterQueryEngine(
    selector=LLMSingleSelector.from_defaults(),
    query_engine_tools=tools,
    verbose=True,
)

response = router_engine.query("What is the parental leave policy?")
# Router picks the HR engine, then answers using it
print(response)
For questions that require answers from multiple engines simultaneously, use
. It selects all relevant engines and merges the answers.
Vector search finds semantically similar chunks but can miss exact keywords. Combining a
with a vector retriever (hybrid search) improves recall for queries containing proper nouns, product names, or specific identifiers.
when the built-in response modes do not fit your use case — for example, returning structured JSON or limiting the response to a fixed word count.
async method for non-blocking integration into async frameworks.

import asyncio
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./docs").load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()

async def main():
    response = await query_engine.aquery("Summarise the key points.")
    print(response)

asyncio.run(main())
to make the query engine return a Pydantic model instead of a string.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex

class DocumentSummary(BaseModel):
    main_topic: str
    key_points: list[str]   # 3-5 bullet points
    sentiment: str           # "positive", "neutral", or "negative"

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(output_cls=DocumentSummary)
response = query_engine.query("Summarise the document.")

summary: DocumentSummary = response.response
print(summary.main_topic)
print(summary.key_points)
Enable streaming to start displaying the synthesised response before the LLM finishes generating. This is especially important for long responses produced by

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(streaming=True)

streaming_response = query_engine.query("Explain vector databases in detail.")

# Option 1: stream to stdout directly
streaming_response.print_response_stream()

# Option 2: yield tokens for a web API
for token in streaming_response.response_gen:
    yield token   # e.g. in a FastAPI StreamingResponse
Measure whether your query engine answers correctly with LlamaIndex's built-in evaluators.
Faithfulness — is the answer grounded in the retrieved context?

from llama_index.core.evaluation import FaithfulnessEvaluator

evaluator = FaithfulnessEvaluator()
query = "What does LlamaIndex stand for?"
response = query_engine.query(query)

result = await evaluator.aevaluate_response(response=response)
print(f"Faithful: {result.passing}")   # True/False
print(f"Feedback: {result.feedback}")
Relevancy — is the retrieved context relevant to the query?

from llama_index.core.evaluation import RelevancyEvaluator

evaluator = RelevancyEvaluator()
result = await evaluator.aevaluate_response(query=query, response=response)
print(f"Relevant: {result.passing}")

from llama_index.core.evaluation import DatasetGenerator, BatchEvalRunner

# Generate evaluation questions from your documents
generator = DatasetGenerator.from_documents(documents[:10])
eval_dataset = await generator.agenerate_dataset_from_nodes(num=20)

# Run all evaluators over the dataset in one pass
runner = BatchEvalRunner(
    {"faithfulness": FaithfulnessEvaluator(), "relevancy": RelevancyEvaluator()},
    workers=4,
)
results = await runner.aevaluate_queries(
    query_engine,
    queries=[d.query for d in eval_dataset.queries.values()],
)

# Print pass rates
for metric, scores in results.items():
    passing = sum(1 for s in scores if s.passing) / len(scores)
    print(f"{metric}: {passing:.0%} pass rate")
Repeated queries with identical prompts waste tokens. LlamaIndex supports prompt-level caching via
— a local disk cache that skips LLM calls when the exact same prompt was seen before.

from llama_index.core import Settings
from llama_index.core.llms.caching import SimpleLLMCache

# Wrap the LLM with the cache
Settings.llm = SimpleLLMCache(
    llm=Settings.llm,
    cache_dir="./llm_cache",
)
With this in place, re-querying the same question in the same Python session (or a new session that reads from the same
directory) returns the cached answer without calling the model.
The source nodes tell you exactly which document chunks contributed to the answer. Logging these in production enables observability dashboards and retrieval quality audits.

response = query_engine.query("What is the data ingestion workflow?")

print(f"Answer: {response.response[:200]}")
print(f"Based on {len(response.source_nodes)} source nodes:")

for i, node in enumerate(response.source_nodes):
    print(f"  [{i}] score={node.score:.3f}")
    print(f"       file={node.metadata.get('file_name', 'unknown')}")
    print(f"       text={node.text[:120]!r}")
API (available in LlamaIndex 0.10+) lets you build arbitrarily complex pipelines with branching, loops, and parallel execution as a DAG.

from llama_index.core.query_pipeline import QueryPipeline, InputComponent
from llama_index.core.postprocessor import SimilarityPostprocessor

retriever = VectorIndexRetriever(index=index, similarity_top_k=10)
postprocessor = SimilarityPostprocessor(similarity_cutoff=0.75)
synthesiser = get_response_synthesizer(response_mode="compact")

pipeline = QueryPipeline(verbose=True)
pipeline.add_modules({
    "input": InputComponent(),
    "retriever": retriever,
    "postprocessor": postprocessor,
    "synthesiser": synthesiser,
})
pipeline.add_link("input", "retriever")
pipeline.add_link("retriever", "postprocessor")
pipeline.add_link("input", "synthesiser", dest_key="query_str")
pipeline.add_link("postprocessor", "synthesiser", dest_key="nodes")

response = pipeline.run(input="Explain query engines in LlamaIndex.")
print(response)
The pipeline graph makes each stage explicit and composable. It also enables automatic parallelism: stages with no shared dependencies run concurrently.
— the default of 2 is fine for demos but misses context in real documents. Start at 5–10 and tune with the faithfulness evaluator.

mode passes the running answer to successive LLM calls. If an early chunk introduces a wrong fact, it can propagate. Run
Query engines orchestrate retrieval, postprocessing, and LLM synthesis in a single pipeline
is the fastest way to query;
decomposes complex questions across multiple data sources automatically
uses an LLM to select the best engine from a set of candidates
Hybrid search (vector + BM25) improves recall for exact-keyword queries — combine retrievers in a
with a Pydantic model to get structured output from any query engine
integrate cleanly into FastAPI and other async frameworks
